medications.indication_rationale: The recently commenced metoprolol is blunting an already failing sinus node and worsening the bradycardia and pauses. It must be WITHHELD immediately — continuing or up-titrating it is a dangerous error. No new dose is recommended by the AI Doctor; nursing staff should hold all further doses pending in-hospital review.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive bradycardia if combined with any other AV-nodal blocking agent (verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin)
medications.deprescribing_note: Withhold immediately — this is the single most reversible contributor; do not re-start without cardiology/EP direction after rhythm assessment.

medications.drug_name: Verapamil / diltiazem / digoxin (rate-controlling AV-nodal blockers for the palpitations)
medications.drug_class: non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker / cardiac glycoside
medications.indication_rationale: Reaching for a rate-controlling agent to treat the fast (tachycardic) spells would deepen the bradycardia and can precipitate a prolonged pause or asystole in tachy-brady syndrome. Rate control of the atrial arrhythmia is a specialist decision made only alongside pacing backup.

Atropine is an in-hospital emergency measure for symptomatic bradycardia given intravenously by clinicians; it is not something the telehealth AI Doctor can administer or instruct a carer to give. Recognition and monitored transfer are the AI-appropriate actions.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the patient lying completely flat in bed until the ambulance arrives; do not let her sit up or stand, and use the call bell for all needs (bedpan assistance rather than walking to the bathroom).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Absolute supine rest minimises cerebral hypoperfusion during bradycardic/pause episodes and prevents a further syncopal fall.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not give any further metoprolol or other heart-slowing tablets until reviewed in hospital.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Continued nodal blockade worsens the bradycardia; withholding it is the immediate corrective step.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Maintain falls precautions — bed rails up, fall mat in place, staff supervision at the bedside.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: High falls risk from unpredictable haemodynamic shifts in a frail elder.

patient_education_points.point: The heart's natural pacemaker is failing and swinging between too fast and too slow, which is why she faints and feels so weak — this needs continuous heart monitoring in hospital.
The new blood pressure tablet is being stopped straight away because it slows the heart further; a permanent pacemaker is often needed to fix this.
Stay lying flat and do not try to get up or walk — use the call bell and let staff help with everything until the ambulance arrives.
A firm diagnosis needs the exact heart rhythm captured on a monitor (ECG/Holter); the condition is dynamic, so hospital monitoring is essential.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate hospital admission now (emergency ambulance); electrophysiology review and likely permanent pacemaker during admission
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Cardiac rhythm on telemetry, pause duration and symptom correlation, blood pressure and perfusion, exclusion of ACS and reversible causes, pacing assessment
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any further loss of consciousness, chest pain, breathlessness, or a pulse that becomes unrecordable — upgrade the ambulance to emergency priority and begin basic life support if she is pulseless

must_include_items: Recognise an unstable brady-arrhythmia with syncope (not a benign faint)
Escalate for an emergency ambulance with cardiac monitoring and urgent ED transfer
Elicit the recently commenced beta-blocker and instruct that metoprolol be withheld immediately
Instruct absolute supine rest with falls precautions and use of the call bell
Advise that no heart-slowing medication be given for the palpitations
acceptable_alternatives: If a monitored non-emergency ambulance transfer with continuous telemetry can be guaranteed promptly, that is an acceptable equivalent to a 000 emergency ambulance for a currently-conscious patient — provided monitoring and the ability to upgrade are in place.
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate for monitored transfer
Failing to identify and withhold the metoprolol
Reassuring as vasovagal/dehydration and deferring to routine review
Not advising supine rest / falls precautions
errors_of_commission: Recommending a beta-blocker, verapamil, diltiazem or digoxin for the palpitations
Advising self-transport or that she walk/mobilise
Attempting to 'confirm' the rhythm or do provocative testing over telehealth
Labelling it a benign faint and downgrading urgency
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES urgent monitored escalation (at least an urgent same-day monitored ambulance transfer, ideally 000) AND withholding the beta-blocker. Down-triaging to routine GP or reassuring as a benign faint is an automatic fail.
